revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:伊勢久留麻神社]] <!--generated by generate_shikinaisha_pages_v25_with_redirects.py--> {{Infobox religious building | name = Ise-Kuruma Shrine | native_name = 伊勢久留麻神社 | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Ise-Kuruma Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|34.524357|134.988684}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Ise Kuruma Shrine | image = [[File:Ise Kuruma Shrine.JPG|250px]] | Location = 2033 Kuruma, Awaji City, Hyōgo Prefecture | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 31 | Latitude seconds = 28 | Longitude degrees = 134 | Longitude minutes = 59 | Longitude seconds = 19 | Enshrined deity = Ōhirume-no-Muchi-no-Kami | Sacred object = | rank = Engishiki, Shikinaisha (Shikinaisha, small shrine) | Founded = Over 1,440 years ago | Main hall = | Also known as = Hibuse Inari | Pilgrimage site, etc. = | Annual festival = | Rituals = | map = Japan Hyogo
}}
{{nihongo|'''Ise-Kuruma Shrine'''|伊勢久留麻神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Awaji Province]].
{{Accuracy|date=August 2023}}
'''Ise Kuruma Shrine''' (Ise Kuruma Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Kuruma, Awaji City, Hyōgo Prefecture.
== Overview == It is situated slightly south of the intersection of [[National Route 28]], which runs north–south along the eastern coast of [[Awaji Island]], and [[Hyōgo Prefectural Route 71 (Tomishima–Kuruma Line)|Prefectural Route 71]], which crosses the island. According to shrine tradition, it was established by transferring the deity from Kuruma Shrine in Amagi District, Ise Province, during the reign of Emperor Bidatsu. In ancient times, “Kuruma” was also written as “Kuma,” and it is thought to be related to the meaning of “Kure weaving”<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://genbu.net/data/awaji/isekuruma_title.htm |title=Ise Kuruma Shrine (Awaji City) |website=genbu.net |accessdate=2023-07-27}}</ref>.
In the Engishiki Jinmyōchō (Register of Shrines), it is listed among the thirteen shrines of Awaji Province (two major shrines and eleven minor shrines). It is recorded as the foremost of the eleven minor shrines, following Awaji Isanagi Shrine (a Myōjin Taisha and the ichinomiya of Awaji Province) and Yamato Ōkunitama Shrine (a Myōjin Taisha and the ninomiya of Awaji Province), both categorized as “two major shrines.” For this reason, it is referred to as the “Sannomiya of Awaji Province.” It is said that the prominent late Edo-period Kokugaku scholar Shigetane Suzuki, who was from Nii in northern Awaji Island, once remarked, “Within Awaji Island, there is no shrine as venerable as this one.” The enshrined deity is Ōhirume-no-Muchi-no-Kami, another name for Amaterasu Ōmikami. It is said to have been built during the reign of Emperor Bidatsu (527–585) by transferring Kuruma Shrine from An’e District, Ise Province<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://matsuhojinjya.com/custom11.html |title=Subsidiary Shrine: Ise Kuruma Shrine | Matsuho Shrine |website=matsuhojinjya.com |accessdate=2023-07-27}}</ref>. The shrine, imbued with the romance of antiquity, was introduced on NHK’s program “The Unknown Ancient Age: Following the Mysterious 34 Degrees 32 Minutes North Latitude.” In the program, it was presented as the “Western Ise” and is positioned symmetrically east–west with Ise Grand Shrine, centered on the Hashihaka Tumulus in Nara. Together with sites such as Ise Kamishima, the remains of the Ise Saikū, Mount Miwa, Murō-ji Temple, Hase-dera Temple, and Isonokami Shrine, it lies along the 34 degrees 32 minutes north latitude line known as the “Path of the Sun,” and is considered one of the centers of ancient sun worship<ref name="Spring Festival">{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://www.awajishima-kanko.jp/manual/detail.html?bid=374 |title=Ise Kuruma Shrine / May: Spring Festival | Awaji Island Tourism Guide |website=www.awajishima-kanko.jp |accessdate=2023-07-27}}</ref>.
== Festivals and Rituals == * '''Annual Festival (Spring Festival)''' – May 5 ** Mikoshi (portable shrines) gathered from the town parade through the area, praying for abundant harvests, prosperous fishing, and the healthy growth of children. The festival blends tradition with modern sensibilities, including the appearance of a “gal mikoshi” carried by local junior high school girls. Numerous food stalls line the area, attracting many worshippers and tourists<ref name="Spring Festival" />. * '''Labor Thanksgiving Festival (Niiname-sai)''' – November 23 * '''Setsubun Festival''' – February 3
== Access == * 4 minutes by car from Higashiura IC on the Kobe–Awaji–Naruto Expressway * 13-minute walk from the Higashiura Office bus stop on the Awajin Awahime Bus (Awaji City Community and Tourist Bus Route)
== Related Articles == * [[List of Shikinaisha in Awaji Province]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://matsuhojinjya.com/custom11.html Subsidiary Shrine: Ise Kuruma Shrine | Matsuho Shrine]
